canonical_definition: CodeParrot is consistently described as a benchmark or dataset for evaluating language models on programming language sequences. The provided literature uses it to measure two related capabilities. One prevalent application is for assessing general language modeling performance, where it is defined as a "code modeling dataset" and performance is measured using perplexity, as shown in one study ("MorphMark"). A more specific framing, found in other work, presents CodeParrot as a "code-oriented long-context benchmark" explicitly used for "length extrapolation evaluation" ("Gated Delta Networks"). In this context, it is used to assess a model's "capacity to extrapolate to sequences of up to 20K tokens." Thus, across the available data, CodeParrot serves as an instrument to evaluate how well models handle code, both in terms of standard language modeling and their ability to generalize to long sequences.
papers: MorphMark: Flexible Adaptive Watermarking for Large Language Models
PlanningArena: A Modular Benchmark for Multidimensional Evaluation of Planning and Tool Learning
Gated Delta Networks: Improving Mamba2 with Delta Rule
Unlocking State-Tracking in Linear RNNs Through Negative Eigenvalues
Wavelet-based Positional Representation for Long Context

definitions.text: A code-oriented long-context benchmark used in the length extrapolation evaluation.
definitions.paper: Gated Delta Networks: Improving Mamba2 with Delta Rule

relationships.paper: Gated Delta Networks: Improving Mamba2 with Delta Rule
relationships.evidence: As shown in Fig.2, we evaluate the models' capacity to extrapolate to sequences of up to 20K tokens across six long-context benchmarks.

relationships.evidence: Language modeling, for which we evaluate perplexity on PG19 (Rae et al., 2020), Proof-Pile (Zhangir Azerbayev), and CodeParrot (CodeParrot) (Section 3.1)
